coredata.dc:title: A potential forecast of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as an ultrasensitive and selective fluorescence probe for Hg (II) ions sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Dhandapani, Elumalai
Maadeswaran, P.
Mohan Raj, R.
Raj, Vairamuthu
Kandiah, Kavitha
Duraisamy, Navaneethan
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are remarkable nanomaterials owing to their auspicious aspects. Herein, we synthesized highly green fluorescent CQDs via a one-step facile hydrothermal method using a chicken feather as a carbon source. The synthesized CQDs have an average particle size of ∼ 5 ± 1 nm in diameter with a quantum yield of ∼ 77.2 %. As-prepared CQDs are directly used as a fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ ions with a detection limit of ∼ 6.2 nM. Interestingly, the above results suggested that the synthesized CQDs as a potential candidate for Hg2+ sensing application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs
Hg2+ sensor
Fluorescence probe
High quantum yield
Green fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from chicken feather
Decoration of CQDs for the sensing of Heavy-Metals
Selectivity and sensitivity of CQDs for Hg2+ detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Dr. Navaneethan Duraisamy acknowledges the DST, New Delhi, India, for the DST Inspire Faculty award (DST/INSPIRE/04/ 2018/001444).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustrations of quantum quenching effect.
a) UV–vis absorption spectra, b) PL spectra, c) The fluorescence emission-excitation 3D map, d) The contour plot of the photoluminescence spectrum of CQDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs with and without various heavy metal ions at the concentration of ∼ 500 μM, (b) Variations in the luminance intensity ratio (F/F0) of CQDs after the addition of various metal ions, (c) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs/Hg2+ system incubated with various concentrations of Hg2+ (0 – 500 μM), (d) Linear plot for fluorescence restoration by Hg2+.
The Quantum Yield of the synthesized CQDs is compared with previous reports.
CQDs probe detection limit of Hg2+ ions compared with previous reports.

head.ce:title: A potential forecast of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as an ultrasensitive and selective fluorescence probe for Hg (II) ions sensing

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: J.K.K. Nataraja College of Arts & Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are remarkable nanomaterials owing to their auspicious aspects. Herein, we synthesized highly green fluorescent CQDs via a one-step facile hydrothermal method using a chicken feather as a carbon source. The synthesized CQDs have an average particle size of ∼ 5 ± 1 nm in diameter with a quantum yield of ∼ 77.2 %. As-prepared CQDs are directly used as a fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ ions with a detection limit of ∼ 6.2 nM. Interestingly, the above results suggested that the synthesized CQDs as a potential candidate for Hg2+ sensing application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nanomaterials get extensive consideration owing to their potential application in various fields such as photocatalysts, bioimaging, sensing, drug delivery, biomedical, tissue engineering, and electrical device applications [1,2]. Nobel metal quantum dots are widely used in the above applications but there are major harmful issues that arise using heavy metal quantum dots. The toxicity is mainly encouraged by the existence of heavy metals. So that the researchers have planned to focus on the synthesis of heavy metal-free quantum dots with excellent fluorescence and biocompatibility. In recent years, carbonaceous materials get more attention owing to their exclusive features such as high chemical stability, optical absorptivity, low toxicity, and biocompatibility [3,4]. The majority of carbonaceous materials are carbon quantum dots (CQDs), nano-diamonds, fullerenes, graphene, and carbon nanotubes. Among this, a very precise consideration is paid more attention to CQDs. Graphene is a sternly 2-D material. Graphene is a single-layered sp2 hybridized carbon atom they are crystalline. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), are the disk of graphene in 2–20 nm size. They are luminous due to the quantum-confinement effect, surface imperfections, and zigzag edges. CQDs are 3-D objects sharing sp3 hybrid bonds. CQDs) are sphere-shaped carbon atoms with a size less than 10 nm. CQDs are typically amorphous and their XRD showed the (100) plane of the basal planar of graphite. The molecular energy level of graphene and CQDs is different, the optical properties of these materials are based on the molecular energy levels (HOMO and LUMO) CQDs consist of CO induces different local distortions which create various energy levels and therefore tune the energy gap. More studies are required to obtain strong experimental evidence for the analysis of the optical properties of the CQDs/GQDs (e.g., the impact of size, surface chemistry, and doping in CQDs/GQDs). Furthermore, detailed analysis is necessary to comprehend how the most common hybrid structure (CQDs/GQDs) interacts with its functional counterparts. In electronic applications like photodetectors or electronic sensors, the presence of organic moieties on CQD surfaces is a significant problem (e.g., non-radiative recombination and bonding with the surrounding atoms). On the other hand, GQDs have the advantage of, a highly conducting structure, while they agonize from low Photoluminescence quantum yield. Fluorescence intensity is a key factor for a luminescence-based sensor for the sensitive and sensitive detection of targets [5,6].

The Quantum Confinement effect (QCE) is the spatial confinement of electron-hole pairs (excitons) in one or more than one dimension in material and also the electronic energy levels are distinct, because of the QCE of the electric wave function to the physical extent of the atoms. it produces exclusive electric and optical assets that have the potential to improve the power conversion efficacy in a photovoltaic device. The energy gap of CQDs is tuned by changing its size, it plays a key role in electronic and optoelectronic materials. The confinement energy of the materials is inversely proportional to the squire of the material size. The simplest 1-D system consists of one particle of mass and length corresponding to m and L, used to explain the QCE. Schrodinger equation is used to examine the wavefunction and energy levels of the 1-D box trapped atom. CQDS are real-life atoms in a box, Despite the particle in a box, which only shows one particle, quantum dots contain two particles (an electron and a hole). Second, the length of the box L is replaced with the radius R since QDs are geometrically spherical rather than square. Due to their interaction with the crystal lattice, the electron and hole's masses are replaced by their effective mass [7,8].

The confinement energy of the electrons in QDs thus becomes:
E =
n
h
2
8
m
e
∗
R
2
+
n
h
2
8
m
h
∗
R
2

The ground state confinement energy of the electrons in QDs becomes:
E =
h
2
8
m
e
∗
R
2
+
h
2
8
m
h
∗
R
2
where E- energy gap, h- Planck’s constant, R-CQDs radius, me *- an effective mass of the electron, and mh*-an effective mass of hole.

These simple models show that the distinct electronic-states rise at conduction (CB) and valence band (VB), and the energy gap of CQDs is size reliant. Therefore, the band gap is inversely proportional to the CQDs, due to the confinement of holes and the electrons. This is known as QCE it is absorbed when the CQD size is close to Bohr radios [9].

CQDs as a novel carbonaceous nanomaterial have drawn an intensifying consideration in current eons, due to their excellent features such as constant photoluminescence (PL) with outstanding optical and electric inexpensive [10]. Based on these outstanding assets, CQDs have been extensively used in several areas, like fluorescent sensors [11], catalysis [12], biomedical imaging, energy storage [13], and light-emitting diodes [14], drug delivery [15], and solar cells [16]. CQDs are employed as an attractive alternative for semiconducting quantum dots, fluorescent dyes, and other fluorescent nanomaterials. There are various synthetic methods used to synthesize CQDs, such as chemical/electrochemical methods [17], arc-discharge [5], pyrolysis [18], laser ablation [19], and hydrothermal/solvothermal techniques [20,21]. Among these synthesis methods, a hydrothermal technique gets more consideration due to its simplicity, cheapness, and eco-friendly. Moreover, various kinds of carbon sources (biomass) had been used to produce CQDs such as vegetables, grass, food waste, fruits, silk, and fish scale [22,23]. In these aspects, massive amounts of chicken feathers are produced each year due to the large consumption of chicken. The preponderance of these feathers is thrown or destroyed as wastage, where only a small percentage is being utilized in fertilizer, biofilm, and insulating goods. Chicken feather is usually cost-free biomass containing rich keratin and crude fat, where employed potential source for CQDs synthesis [24,25]. Chicken feathers encompassed around 97 % keratin. The hollow, microcrystalline, oriented keratin feather fibers offer a novel, low-cost approach to producing carbon fibers through controlled pyrolysis. keratin gains chemical and thermal stability due to its intermolecular bonding, including non-covalent hydrogen bonds, ionic salt bonds, and covalent disulfide cystine crosslinking. Chicken feather waste in the agricultural industry is increasing due to the higher demand for chickens and has become a promising candidate for porous carbon with a high capacity. So, the chicken feathers as used as a carbon resource for the synthesis of carbonaceous materials [26,27].

Heavy Metal ions are a serious global issue due to their severe hazards from various industrial wastes, it affects living organisms and severe environmental pollution. Heavy metal ions are not easy to convert into non-toxic products like other toxic pollutants [28]. Highly toxic heavy metal ions are mercury (Hg2+), cadmium (Cd2+), and lead (Pb2+) arsenic (As), which exhibited toxicity at low concentrations. The heavy metal ions are accumulated in living organisms in several ways (drinking, breathing, etc), Heavy metals are penetrated tissues and organs (bound with nucleic acid, and proteins) that gradually demolished the macro-molecules and interrupt their cellular processes. It causes serious issues in living organisms such as heart diseases, kidney diseases, respiratory problems, central nervous system, brain damage, etc. So, the detection of heavy metal ions significantly prevents the consumption of heavy metals that control diseases [29,30]. Nowadays many techniques are available to detect heavy metal ions such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [31], atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [32], UV–vis-detectors [33], electrothermal atomic absorption spectroscopy (ET-AAS) [34], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [35], etc. the above methods are detecting the metal ions with high accuracy and speed. Among these methods, the fluorescent probe technique gets more consideration due to its advantages of quick analyses, easy process, and high sensitivity. Currently, CQDs are used as a fluorescent probe for the detection and analysis of metal ions, based on the reaction between the metals and the CQDs modified probe. In low quantities, heavy metals were frequently necessary and occasionally harmful to human health, but their accretion may cause a wide range of chronic disorders. Heavy metal contamination was mostly triggered by Hg2+, Pb2+, As2+, Cu2+, and Cd2+, which were observed the most serious environmental issues, with the potential to threaten the entire planet's sustainability. In this aspect, the development of adaptable technologies to enable real-time detection of heavy metals was an important eye-opening target for researchers and scientists. CQDs are an attractive candidate for portable sensors, due to their availability, cost-effectiveness, and solubility.

Herein, we designated the chicken feather as a carbon source for CQD synthesis. It is a potential alternative for other fluorescent sensing materials to the selective detection of heavy metals in the environment. To the best of our knowledge, there are no articles published based on the chicken feather as source material for CQDs synthesis and used to detect metal ions via photoluminescence quenching intensity of synthesized CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from chicken feather
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs synthesis from chicken feathers via single-step hydrothermal method. Hence, the chicken feather was collected from the local chicken shop and washed with tap water followed by deionized water, and dried overnight at 60 °C. the dried feathers were slashed using an electric blender and weighing 5 g of chopped feathers was added to 100 mL of deionized water and stirred well and the mixture was transferred into a 150 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor. The hydrothermal reactor was placed in a hot air oven at 150 ˚C for 24 h and then cooled. The resulting brown color solution was filtered using Whatman filter paper with a pore size of 0.2 µm, and the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min, the resultant supernatant solution was stockpiled at 4 °C for further characterization. The Schematic illustration of CQD synthesis is shown in Fig. 1 .

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of CQDs were analyzed by a D-8 advanced diffractometer, Brucker Germany with Cu-Kα as a radiation source. Surface functional groups of the synthesized samples were characterized by (the KBr pellet method) using Nicolet-6700 spectrometer (FTIR). UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs was recorded by JASCO V-570. Photoluminescence (PL) emission/excitation spectra were carried out by Jasco PF-770. The structural evaluation and size of CQDs were determined by a Transmission electron microscope (TEM) under 200 kV acceleration voltage (TEM, Hitachi).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Decoration of CQDs for the sensing of Heavy-Metals
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized feather carbon quantum dots are used as a standard to detect various kinds of metal ions in the concentration of ∼ 500 µM including Cd2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, and Hg2. The reaction was performed using ultra-pure deionized water, 2 mL of CQDs, and 2 mL of metal ions from the stock solution were used to record the PL spectra for the selective detection of the heavy metal ion. The selectively detected metal ion concentration will be changed to various concentrations for the sensitive detection of Hg2+. As a result of the quantum confinement effect, the binding and interface amid the heavy metals and probes causes changes in the physicochemical properties of fluorophores, such as emission intensity, long life, and microstructures, as well as offers a constructive signal which can selectively signify solutes with great sensitivity. The schematic illustrations of quantum quenching effect are revealed in Fig. 2 .

As-prepared CQDs quantum yield (QY) was examined by a quinine sulfate (QY ∼ 53 %) as a reference in 0.5 M H2SO4 at 380 nm excitation. The excitation wavelength and the widths were the same for both Quinine sulfate and CQDs. The QY of the synthesized CQDs was calculated using the following equation [36].
(1)
ϕ
CDs
=
ϕ
Ref
∗
I
CDs
/
I
Ref
∗
η
CDs
2
/
η
Ref
2
∗
A
Ref
/
A
CDs
Where Ф - QY, I - enclosed integrated area of PL, η - solvent refractive index, and A – absorbance. The QY comparison with previous reports is listed in Table 1 .

UV–vis spectra of synthesized CQDs are illustrated in Fig. 3 (a), this curve displays two major peaks a robust absorption band at 273 nm related to the conjugated aromatic π-π* transition of sp2 (CC) hybrid orbitals, and a weak absorption shoulder at 330 nm is corresponding to characteristic n-π* transitions of sp3 (CO/CN) hybrid orbitals [37,38]. Further, the Photoluminescence (PL) spectrum is exemplified in Fig. 3(b-d). PL spectra are recorded at various excitation wavelengths from 320 to 430 nm with 10 nm intervals, and sensitive emission of CQDs is analyzed via various excitation. CQDs showed the highest emission at 401 nm corresponding to the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Increasing excitation wavelength leads to the moving of CQDs emission into monotonic redshift, which denotes the existence of photon re-absorption [39–42]. These deeds can be assumed, since the quantum-confinement effect, CQD size is inversely proportional to the energy gap. So, the energy gap is wider for smaller size particles, the excitation of photon energy is steadily reduced, and smaller size particles with greater energy gaps, can't be aroused, thus they don't assist PL any longer, resulting that the total PL is in a redshift [43,44].

The surface-functional groups of synthesized CQDs are investigated by FT-IR spectra, Fig. 4 insisting on the existence of OH stretching vibration band at 3380 cm−1 and 1044 cm−1 , methyl (CH3) and ethyl (CH2) CH stretching vibration bands are surviving at 2926 cm−1 and 2858 cm−1 , respectively [45]. The peak at 2363 cm−1 corresponds to the CN band, and a strong and sharp at 1653 cm−1 is allocated to the symmetry CO stretching vibration of COO–. The manifestation of amine functionality on the CQD surface is established via a peak at 1389 cm−1. assigned as NH stretching vibration [46–48]. The peak at 651 cm−1 is attributed to the CH/CC in-plane bending/stretching vibration of aryl/alkyl (C6H5) groups. The synthesized CQD surface is passivated by oxygen (O2) atoms, passivation elucidates that the CQDs are well soluble in water [49]. According to earlier research, the surface edge/bonding structure of CQDs has a major impact on their electrical assets and luminescence capabilities. Hence, the knowledge gained here concerning the structural features of CQDs can aid in the understanding of their illumination mechanics.

Fig. 5 elucidates the XRD pattern of CQDs, it showed a strong and broad peak at 2θ ∼ 26.3 corresponding to the (111) plane with JCPDS No, 75–0444. The lattice d spacing value is ∼ 3.3571 Å, analogous to the graphitic edifice of CQDs. The noiseless broad peak establishes the synthesized CQD samples exist as amorphous [50,51].

Fig. 6 illustrates the TEM image of synthesized CQD with a well-illustration of particle size and surface morphology. The CQDs displayed nearly-spherical morphology with a calculated average particle size of 5 ± 1 nm in diameter [52,53]. The size distribution ranges from 3 to 9.5 nm with an average diameter of 5.0 ± 1 nm [54].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of CQDs for Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the synthesized CQDs towards various metal ions is studied with the aid of the following materials Cd2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ 500 μM concentration of these metal ions is added to the CQDs solution, and the fluorescence responses are recorded (Fig. 7 (a)). Further, Fig. 7(b) revealed that the addition of Hg2+ triggered a drastic decay in the CQDs fluorescence intensity, and other metals such as Cd2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, and Pb2+ exhibited insignificant effects except for Fe3+, Pb2+, it suggests that the interaction between the Hg2+ and CQDs is very strong. Therefore, the CQDs exposed outstanding selectivity for the detection of Hg2+.

As synthesized highly green fluorescent CQDs via a simple one-step hydrothermal method using a chicken feather as a carbon source has a quantum yield of ∼ 77.2 %. The CQDs act as a highly effective and sensitive probe for the selective detection of Hg2+. The luminescence of CQDs can be quenched by Hg2+, and dynamic quenching is the predominant quenching strategy. Synthesized CQDs have a LOD of 6.2 nM. The detection approach has several advantages, including the use of green-raw materials, inexpensive, rapid response time, and ease of customization, which indicate a lot of potential for the detection of Hg2+ in an aqueous solution. CQDs are potential options for monitoring and regulating environmental contamination due to their great features and appealing applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Dr. Navaneethan Duraisamy acknowledges the DST, New Delhi, India, for the DST Inspire Faculty award (DST/INSPIRE/04/ 2018/001444).
